metadata.title: Analyze DAG Paths with Claude and NetworkX

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?
props.segment: Analyze DAG Paths with Claude and

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Every causal claim has a path story behind it. When you draw a DAG — a directed acyclic graph — you are saying that causation flows along certain edges. But not every path from treatment to outcome is causal. Some are back-door paths through a common cause. Some pass through a collider and open when you condition on it. The question this video answers: how do you enumerate every path, classify each one, and use d-separation to decide which ones you need to block.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'paths in a DAG — causal vs back-door vs collider'; stakes for identification; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. In the terminal: build a Python script using NetworkX that takes a DAG as an edge list, enumerates all paths from treatment to outcome, classifies each path as directed causal, back-door, or collider, applies d-separation rules, and calls Claude for a plain English identification verdict.

props.command: claude "Build a Python script using NetworkX: (1) takes a DAG as edge list; (2) enumerates all paths from treatment to outcome; (3) classifies paths as directed-causal, back-door, or collider; (4) applies d-separation rules; (5) calls Claude for plain-English identification verdict"

beats.narration_text: Read it. The core is one NetworkX call: all simple paths from treatment to outcome. NetworkX gives you every route — directed and undirected traversals both. The classification logic checks each path node by node: is the middle node a fork, a chain, or a collider? A fork with an unblocked common cause is a back-door path. A collider is blocked by default and opens when you condition.

props.code:
# dag_analyzer.py — Claude Code output
import networkx as nx

def analyze_paths(G, treatment, outcome):
    paths = list(nx.all_simple_paths(
        G, source=treatment, target=outcome
    ))
    for path in paths:
        kind = classify_path(G, path)
        print(f'{" -> ".join(path)}: {kind}')

# Example: Smoking -> Tar -> Cancer
# Back-door: Smoking <- Genotype -> Cancer
# d-sep: block Genotype to identify effect

beats.narration_text: Run it on the smoking DAG. Four nodes: Smoking, Tar, Cancer, Genotype. The green path — Smoking to Tar to Cancer — is the directed causal path. That is what you want to measure. The orange path — Smoking back through Genotype to Cancer — is a back-door path. Genotype is a common cause of both smoking and cancer. Block Genotype and the identification is clean.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: animated DAG with Smoking-Tar-Cancer causal path in green, Smoking-Genotype-Cancer back-door in orange, d-separation verdict at bottom

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to extend the script: add a collider detection step and show what happens when you accidentally condition on Tar — a mediator on the causal path. Conditioning on a mediator blocks the path you want and gives you a biased estimate of the direct effect.

props.command:
claude "update dag_analyzer.py:
  -> add mediator conditioning warning
  -> show what conditioning on Tar does to identification
  -> print updated d-sep verdict"

beats.narration_text: The updated output flags the mediator conditioning issue. Conditioning on Tar blocks the Smoking to Tar to Cancer path — the only causal path. The identification verdict changes from identified to blocked-by-mediator. The script now distinguishes between blocking a back-door path, which is required, and blocking a causal path, which destroys your estimate.
beats.visual_intent: slate: updated terminal output showing mediator warning and revised identification verdict

beats.narration_text: So a DAG is not just a picture. It is a set of independence claims. Every path is either a channel of association or a blocked one. The back-door criterion asks: can you find a set of variables that blocks all back-door paths without opening any colliders and without touching any descendants of treatment? If yes, you have identification.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: three path types — causal (green), back-door (orange), collider (blocked by default); back-door criterion stated

beats.narration_text: Your move: take any domain where you have a treatment and an outcome and list every variable you can think of that might connect them. Draw the DAG in NetworkX as an edge list and run this script. The next reel builds on this — it implements the full back-door criterion solver, not just path enumeration.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) draw your own DAG, 2) run path analysis, 3) proceed to back-door criterion solver

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. So a DAG is not just a picture. It is a set of independence claims. Every path is either a channel of association or a blocked one. The back-door criterion asks: can you find a set of variables that blocks all back-door paths without opening any colliders and without touching any descendants of treatment? If yes, you have identification. Your move: take any domain where you have a treatment and an outcome and list every variable you can think of that might connect them. beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Analyze DAG Paths with Claude and NetworkX] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
